---
title: "Creating a Conda Environment"
---

## Why Should I Use Conda?
Conda is an open-source package and environment management system that simplifies software installation and version control. With Conda, you can:

* Install and manage software (including **Python** and **R** packages) along with all their dependencies

* Create isolated environments so different projects can use different versions of the same software without conflicts.

* Reproduce analyses easily since an environment file (`environment.yml`) can capture the exact software versions used in your workflow.

This makes conda especially useful for computational biology and single-cell RNA-seq analysis where software dependencies can be complex and version-sensitive.

:::{.callout-note title="Conda vs renv (Python vs R origins)"}
Although Conda was originally popularized for **Python** workflows, it can also manage **R** environments (and many non-Python tools).  
However, for R-specific projects, many users prefer **renv** for its tight integration with R and CRAN/Bioconductor packages.  
In mixed R + Python projects, a common pattern is:

- Use **renv** to lock down **R** packages for the R portion of the analysis, and
- Use **conda** (or another Python environment manager) for **Python** dependencies (often accessed from R via *reticulate*).
If your workflow includes both R and Python components, you may find it helpful to use both tools in tandem. To learn more about renv, see our [renv tutorial](/pages/resources/renv.qmd).
:::

:::{.callout-note title="Installing Conda"}
To install Conda, follow the [Miniconda installation instructions](https://www.anaconda.com/docs/getting-started/miniconda/install).  
**Miniconda** is the lightweight version of Anaconda and is recommended for most users.
:::

## Create a New Environment
There are many ways to organize your Conda environments, depending on your workflow and software dependencies.
As a general rule, you should create a dedicated Conda environment for each project to prevent version conflicts and ensure reproducibility.

To create a conda environment with Python, type into the command line:

```bash
conda create --name myenv
```

and replace `myenv` with your desired environment name. Then, to activate your environment:

```bash
conda activate myenv
```

Now, anything you run in this shell will use only the packages that you have explicitly installed. To deactivate your environment, run:

```bash
conda deactivate
```

## Installing a Package with Conda
To install a package with Conda, you usually have to **specify a channel** — an online repository where packages are stored and distributed. When you install a package with Conda, it looks for that package in one or more channels (for example, defaults, conda-forge, or bioconda).

* **defaults**  — maintained by Anaconda; includes general-purpose packages.  

* **conda-forge** - a community-maintained channel providing up-to-date, cross-platform scientific software.

* **bioconda** — specializes in bioinformatics software and builds on conda-forge for its dependencies.

When installing bioinformatics tools like `kb-python` or `seqspec`, it’s best practice to specify both conda-forge and bioconda as channels to ensure compatibility:

```bash
conda install -c conda-forge -c bioconda kb-python
```

## How to Save and Restore an Old Environment
You can save your conda environment in a **YAML** file with:
```bash
conda env export --from-history > environment.yml
```
The `--from-history` flag saves only the packages you explicitly installed, keeping the file concise.
If you prefer to include all dependencies and versions, omit this flag.

To recreate the same environment elsewhere:
```bash
conda env create -f environment.yml --name myenv
```
## Using Conda Environments with JupyterLab and Jupyter Notebooks
Most Scanpy workflows are run interactively in **JupyterLab** or **Jupyter notebooks**. To use a conda environment in JupyterLab, you need to register it as a kernel. This allows you to select that environment when creating or running notebooks.

### Registering a conda environment as a Jupyter kernel
First, from your project environment:

```bash
conda activate myenv
conda install -c conda-forge ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user --name myenv --display-name "Python (myenv)"
```

Then start JupyterLab from wherever you normally run it. The **Python (myenv)** kernel will still be available.

### Quick sanity checks
Inside a notebook cell, confirm you are using the expected environment:

```python
import sys
print(sys.executable)
```

You can also confirm that Scanpy is being imported from the environment you expect:

```python
import scanpy as sc
print(sc.__version__)
```

### Removing a kernel (optional)
If you no longer need the kernel registration:

```bash
jupyter kernelspec list
jupyter kernelspec uninstall myenv
```
